change_of_doctoral_degree.new_doctoral_d.answer: Doctor of Philosophy in Biochemistry and Biotechnology

change_of_doctoral_degree.motivation.answer:
When I initially enrolled in the Doctor of Philosophy in Biology programme in October 2024, my proposed project was broadly focused on plant ecology and climate resilience. Over the past year and a half, my research interests and the actual day-to-day work in the lab have shifted significantly toward molecular and biochemical questions, particularly around gene regulation of stress responses in Arabidopsis and barley. 

Concretely, the project that my supervisor and I have now developed is centred on the biochemical characterization of drought-response transcription factors and their downstream metabolic pathways. This involves extensive work in molecular cloning, protein expression and purification, enzyme kinetics, and metabolomics profiling—methodologies and theoretical frameworks that align much more closely with the Doctor of Philosophy in Biochemistry and Biotechnology than with the more organismal and ecological orientation of the Biology programme.

Over the last academic year, I have:
- Completed advanced courses in enzymology, structural biology, and systems biology offered within the Biochemistry and Biotechnology curriculum, with grades of 16/20 or higher.
- Spent the majority of my research hours in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology laboratory, working alongside other PhD students who are enrolled in the Biochemistry and Biotechnology programme.
- Co-authored a manuscript (currently under revision) that focuses on the biochemical interaction between a specific NAC transcription factor and a subset of promoters controlling osmoprotectant biosynthesis—again, firmly situated within biochemical and biotechnological research.

My daily supervision is now primarily conducted by co-supervisor Prof. Dr. Tom Janssens from the Department of Biochemistry and Microbiology, and my work is embedded in his research group rather than in the ecology-focused team where I originally started. During our regular progress meetings, both Prof. Janssens and my main supervisor, Prof. Dr. Ann De Smet (Department of Biology), have repeatedly noted that the scientific questions, techniques, and publication targets of my project are aligned with biochemistry and biotechnology doctoral outcomes.

From a career perspective, I aim to continue in an academic or industrial R&D environment focusing on crop improvement through molecular breeding and gene editing. Having my doctoral degree formally situated in Biochemistry and Biotechnology will more accurately reflect the expertise I am building (molecular cloning, protein biochemistry, high-throughput metabolite analysis, and functional genomics) and will make my profile clearer to future employers and collaborators. Several of the industry partners we are in contact with (e.g. a plant biotechnology company in Ghent and a seed company in the Netherlands) have already expressed that a PhD in Biochemistry and Biotechnology better captures the competencies required for the positions I am interested in.

Practically, the switch will also remove some administrative inconsistencies that have arisen over the past year. For example, I have been taking doctoral training activities (specialized seminars and workshops) listed under the Biochemistry and Biotechnology programme, and there have been repeated questions from the faculty administration about how to properly register these within the Biology doctoral trajectory. Aligning the official doctoral degree with the actual scientific and training context will simplify the administration for both my supervisors and the faculty student administration.

Both my main supervisor and co-supervisor strongly support this change. We have discussed the implications for my doctoral advisory committee, publication plan, and timing for the doctoral training programme. The scientific content of my project will remain the same, but the evaluation criteria and expectations will be better matched to the biochemical and biotechnological nature of the work. This should lead to a more coherent doctoral trajectory, clearer communication with the faculty, and a degree title that accurately reflects my expertise at the time of graduation.
